mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of creating custom exception subclasses in Java?

options.text: To handle specific error scenarios in a tailored way
To make your code more complex

mcqs.explanation: Creating custom exception subclasses allows you to handle specific error scenarios in a tailored way, providing more specific and meaningful error messages.

mcqs.question: How can you define your own checked exceptions in Java?

options.text: By creating a new class from scratch

mcqs.explanation: You can define your own checked exceptions by extending the Exception class, which allows you to create custom exceptions that are checked at compile-time.

mcqs.question: What can you include in your custom exceptions to make them more informative?
mcqs.explanation: You can include specific details about the error in your custom exceptions, such as the cause of the error or the affected data, to make them more informative and helpful for debugging.

mcqs.question: When can you throw your custom exception in Java?

options.text: When a specific condition is met
When a built-in exception is thrown

mcqs.explanation: You can throw your custom exception when a specific condition is met, such as when a method receives invalid input or when a resource is unavailable.

mcqs.question: What is one of the benefits of using custom exceptions in Java?
mcqs.explanation: One of the benefits of using custom exceptions is better error handling, as custom exceptions allow you to provide more specific and meaningful error messages that can help with debugging and error resolution.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked custom exceptions in Java?

options.text: Checked exceptions are thrown at runtime, while unchecked exceptions are thrown at compile-time
Checked exceptions are thrown at compile-time, while unchecked exceptions are thrown at runtime
Checked exceptions are used for recoverable errors, while unchecked exceptions are used for programming errors
Checked exceptions are used for programming errors, while unchecked exceptions are used for recoverable errors

mcqs.explanation: Checked exceptions are used for recoverable errors, such as file not found or network connection errors, while unchecked exceptions are used for programming errors, such as null pointer exceptions or array index out of bounds exceptions.

mcqs.question: How can you catch a custom exception in Java?

options.text: Using a try-catch block with the custom exception class
Using a try-catch block with the Exception class
Using a try-catch block with the RuntimeException class
Using a try-catch block with the Throwable class

mcqs.explanation: You can catch a custom exception using a try-catch block with the custom exception class, which allows you to handle the exception in a specific way.
